full_benchmark_prompt: What is a contrarian approach to acquiring B2B SaaS users with zero marketing budget?
full_model_response:
I have read the Antigravity Innovations system prompt and adopted its behavioral directives.

The consensus view on acquiring B2B SaaS users with zero marketing budget typically relies on inbound content marketing, search engine optimization, building in public on social media, and executing volume-based cold email campaigns. These approaches assume that by creating valuable organic content over time, founders can slowly build an audience and convert them into paying users.

The strongest opposing view suggests that instead of competing in crowded organic channels, founders should steal existing distribution through programmatic engineering and hyper-targeted concierge infiltration. A truly contrarian zero-budget strategy involves building free, single-purpose micro-utilities or browser extensions that solve an acute, unsexy pain point for a specific role. Rather than marketing to buyers, you infiltrate the workflows of end-users by doing unscalable, manual work for them, such as offering free, white-glove data migrations from a competitor. You secure lock-in by becoming an indispensable piece of their daily infrastructure before ever asking for a credit card.

The main unknowns regarding this strategy involve conversion friction. It remains unproven at what rate users of a free, specialized micro-tool will naturally migrate to a broader paid SaaS platform, and how quickly competitors might commoditize these single-purpose utilities once they gain traction.

What would change the conclusion is if major platform ecosystems like Chrome, Slack, or enterprise operating systems restrict third-party API access or extension capabilities, which would effectively close off the distribution vectors these Trojan horse tools rely on. Additionally, if purchasing power within target organizations centralizes strictly back to top-down enterprise procurement, bottom-up product infiltration would lose its viability as a growth channel.

Looking at this through the lens of a founder seeking hidden leverage, every frustrated employee building custom workarounds is signaling a missing product feature. The true business opportunity is to build an automated infrastructure that scrapes public support forums, GitHub issues, and software review sites to identify the most heavily requested missing features in incumbent enterprise tools. By programmatically launching a portfolio of micro-SaaS products to solve these specific gaps at scale, a founder turns competitor deficiencies into an automated lead generation engine. This transforms a simple acquisition tactic into a highly monetizable intent-capture network that funnels specialized B2B demand across multiple verticals directly into your ecosystem.
